metadata.name: Module 7
metadata.description: Earth observation exercise over the Strait of Malacca and Hawaii. LEO pass with global ground-station coverage and natural contact gaps between passes.

questions.title: What should the Mission Lead confirm at the start of each ground-station pass?
questions.description: At pass acquisition, the Mission Lead sets the team up for a successful contact window. Select the best answer.

options: Begin payload capture immediately
Confirm contact, pass timeline and ground station
Rotate encryption keys before any telemetry review
Safe-mode the spacecraft until imagery is requested
reason: Payload capture should wait until link quality and pass plan are confirmed.
Correct. The Mission Lead briefs contact status, the pass window, which ground station is in use, and what the team must accomplish before commanding begins.
Key rotation is not the standard first action at pass start.
Safe-mode is an anomaly response, not a nominal pass-start procedure.

questions.title: Which action should the Mission Lead approve personally rather than delegate?
questions.description: Some steps are delegated to Satellite or Payload Operations; others need Mission Lead authority. Select the best answer.

options: Configuring camera field-of-view before capture
Entering safe-mode or authorising a spacecraft reset
Routine nadir pointing for a standard capture
Scheduled telemetry downlink during a good pass
reason: Camera configuration is a standard Payload Operations task once tasking is accepted.
Correct. Safe-mode and reset decisions affect the whole pass plan and should be approved by the Mission Lead.
Routine pointing is normally executed by Satellite or Payload Operations once tasking is approved.
Nominal downlink during a healthy pass is typically delegated.

questions.title: What should the Mission Lead review immediately after a pass ends?
questions.description: When the contact window closes, the Mission Lead closes out the pass with the team. Select the best answer.

options: Immediate safe-mode regardless of pass outcome
Only the spacecraft battery voltage trend
Whether primary tasking was completed and if any anomalies occurred
Whether the camera aperture should be changed before the next orbit
reason: Safe-mode is not the default end-of-pass action when operations were nominal.
Battery telemetry alone is insufficient for an end-of-pass review.
Correct. After each pass the Mission Lead confirms tasking status, captures anomalies, and sets expectations for the next contact opportunity.
Camera settings are a Payload Operations detail, not the primary end-of-pass brief.

questions.title: What is the correct first action at pass acquisition?
questions.description: Satellite Operations owns the link during each contact window. Select the nominal first step.

options: Capture imagery of the tasked region
Downlink all stored data immediately
Reconfigure the on-board command schedule
Verify link with station and monitor SNR
reason: Capture is a Payload Operations step after the link is healthy.
Downlink may follow once the pass plan is confirmed, not as the first action.
Schedule changes are not the standard acquisition step.
Correct. Acquire the link, confirm SNR is acceptable, and monitor telemetry before handing off or supporting payload tasking.

questions.title: Which conditions should cause Satellite Operators to notify the Mission Lead?
questions.description: Select all telemetry-based triggers that should stop routine commanding until the Mission Lead is informed.

options: Battery charge fraction is nominal
Routine ping received on schedule
SNR falls below the mission threshold
Spacecraft enters SAFE mode unexpectedly
Uplink command is not acknowledged
reason: Link degradation, unexpected safe-mode, and missing command acks all require a pause and Mission Lead brief. Routine pings and nominal battery telemetry do not.

questions.title: What should Satellite Operations do when the contact window is about to end?
questions.description: LEO passes are short and the link will drop. Select the best nominal end-of-pass action.

options: Begin a new imagery capture sequence in the final seconds of the pass
Brief the Mission Lead on pass status and stop non-essential commands
Continue issuing new commands until the link drops
Reset the spacecraft computer automatically at pass end
reason: Starting capture too late in the window risks incomplete products and wasted power.
Correct. Satellite Operations monitors the pass timeline, hands status to the Mission Lead, and avoids starting work that cannot finish before link loss.
Silent command flooding at pass end creates risk and leaves the team without situational awareness.
Computer reset is an approved anomaly response, not a routine pass-end procedure.

questions.title: How many yellow vessels are in the Strait of Malacca?
questions.description: Use Earth observation imagery of the Strait of Malacca region and count the number of yellow vessels.

answer.reason: Four yellow vessels are located in the Strait of Malacca.

questions.title: How many green vessels are around Hawaii?
questions.description: Use Earth observation imagery of the Hawaii region and count the number of green vessels.

answer.reason: Five green vessels are located around Hawaii.

questions.title: What must Payload Operations verify before releasing a captured product?
questions.description: After capture and downlink, Payload Operations checks the product before delivery. Select the best answer.

options: Image quality, correct target region, and tasking metadata
Only that the downlink completed without error
That the next ground-station pass is scheduled
The current encryption key value
reason: Correct. Payload Operations verifies the imagery quality, that the correct geography was captured, and that tasking metadata matches the request.
A completed downlink alone does not confirm the product is usable.
Future pass scheduling is not part of the product release check.
Encryption key is a communications concern, not a product-quality gate.

questions.title: Which findings should cause Payload Operations to hold a downlinked product as suspect?
questions.description: Select all integrity triggers that should stop a product from being delivered until it is reviewed.

options: Capture geometry does not match the tasked region
Ground-station SNR was acceptable at pass start
Image appears corrupted or incomplete
Routine ping telemetry is nominal
Vessel count or colour in imagery contradicts the tasking brief
reason: Corrupted imagery, wrong geography, and count/colour mismatches are all reasons to hold a product. Nominal pings and acceptable pass-start SNR do not clear a suspect product on their own.
